agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given area/time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "The standard delivery price before any dynamic adjustment is applied (implied but not stated)" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to the base price as a function of current demand" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted based on current demand levels — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. The direction of correlation (demand↑ → price↑) is a business strategy decision, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to offer loss-leader pricing during high demand for market share.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D1 > D2 in the same zone/time context, the computed price for D1 must be >= the computed price for D2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — prices reflect current demand, not stale or batched demand data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a performance/freshness policy. The business could decide hourly recalculation is acceptable. This is an SLA, not a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The time lag between a demand signal change and the corresponding price update must be below a defined threshold (threshold unspecified by CEO)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no manual intervention required for routine price adjustments",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is an operational policy. The business could introduce manual approval gates for certain price tiers without corrupting the system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are computed and applied programmatically without requiring human approval in the standard flow"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values — both a ceiling (max price) and floor (min price) must be enforced",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, an automated system could produce zero or negative prices (giving away service or paying customers to take deliveries) or astronomical prices that constitute price gouging. Either extreme corrupts business integrity. The CEO explicitly calls this out as necessary. A price must always be a positive, bounded value.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed dynamic price P: MIN_PRICE <= P <= MAX_PRICE, where MIN_PRICE > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and commercially nonsensical — the business is providing a service that has real cost. This is a fundamental domain constraint independent of any policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price emitted by the DynaPricing system must be strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The pricing strategy must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices should not systematically fall below cost",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic business decision that leadership can tune. Temporarily pricing below cost for market capture is a valid business choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Over a configurable evaluation window, average dynamic price must meet or exceed average delivery cost (thresholds undefined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The price shown to a customer at booking time must be the price charged — no retroactive price changes after acceptance",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a fundamental commercial and legal constraint in any real-time pricing system. Once a customer accepts a quoted price, changing it retroactively breaks contractual integrity and likely violates consumer protection law. The CEO doesn't state it because it's assumed — but it is a hard invariant of any pricing domain.",
      "testable_assertion": "Once a delivery is booked at price P, the charged amount equals P regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Demand measurement must have a defined geographic and temporal scope — demand is always relative to a zone and time window",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "How demand is scoped (city-wide vs neighborhood, 5-min window vs 1-hour) is a tunable policy decision, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every demand calculation is associated with a specific geographic zone and time window"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert ends, natural disaster)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrail ceiling prevents price from exceeding MAX_PRICE; price ramps up but is capped. Natural disaster scenarios may require regulatory compliance (anti-price-gouging laws)." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero in a zone", "expected_behavior": "Price falls to the floor (MIN_PRICE) but never below it; system continues to quote valid prices even with no demand." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is shown a price, but demand changes before they confirm booking", "expected_behavior": "Business must decide: honor the quoted price for a TTL window, or re-quote. Either way, once booked, the price is locked (BR-7)." },
    { "scenario": "System outage or demand data feed becomes unavailable", "expected_behavior": "Fallback pricing behavior is undefined. System must not emit zero or null prices. A sensible default (e.g., last known price or base price) is needed." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers in the same zone at the same millisecond get different prices due to a demand threshold crossing", "expected_behavior": "This is commercially acceptable in dynamic pricing, but the system must be deterministic — same inputs produce same price." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values (MIN/MAX) are misconfigured (e.g., MIN > MAX)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configuration and refuse to start/apply pricing with contradictory bounds." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory jurisdiction has anti-price-gouging laws during declared emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by CEO. Dynamic pricing during emergencies is a legal and reputational risk that must be addressed." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing captures surplus during high demand and maintains volume during low demand. Guardrail misconfiguration could cause revenue loss (floor too high kills volume) or margin erosion (ceiling too low leaves money on the table).",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches. Adds operational complexity vs static pricing.",
    "user": "Customers experience variable pricing which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price perception is critical — customers who see a high price and wait may game the system.",
    "risk": "Regulatory risk from anti-price-gouging laws in certain jurisdictions. Reputational risk if prices spike during emergencies or crises. Algorithmic risk if demand signals are noisy or manipulated.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices; may perceive unfairness. Need transparency on why prices change." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Higher demand pricing may increase their compensation if tied to delivery fees; undefined whether surge revenue flows to them." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Must model revenue under variable pricing; need visibility into guardrail effectiveness and margin impact." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and fair pricing requirements per jurisdiction." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; need scripts and policies for handling disputes." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Must build real-time infrastructure, demand modeling, guardrail management, and price display UX." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement with almost no specifics. Critical parameters are undefined: demand measurement methodology, guardrail values, price floor/ceiling, geographic/temporal granularity, update frequency definition of 'real time', cost basis for profitability, surge multiplier curves, quote TTL, fallback behavior, regulatory compliance approach, and courier revenue sharing. Only the directional intent (demand drives price, automated, guardrails exist) is clear. This is far too vague to build from without significant follow-up.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices must be positive (BR-5) — universal domain truth",
      "Price locked at booking (BR-7) — fundamental commercial constraint",
      "Demand and price are positively correlated (BR-1) — explicitly stated",
      "Guardrails must exist (BR-4) — explicitly stated"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'real time' — seconds? minutes? event-driven?",
      "Guardrail values and how they are managed",
      "How demand is measured, scoped, and weighted",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements per jurisdiction",
      "Fallback behavior during system degradation",
      "Whether price quotes have a TTL before re-quoting"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists that the dynamic adjustment modifies (not stated)",
      "Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling (CEO only said 'don't go crazy' implying ceiling, but floor is also needed)",
      "Price is locked once a customer books (standard commercial practice, not stated)",
      "Demand is measured per geographic zone and time window (not stated but required for any practical implementation)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO's description lacks critical specifics: What defines 'demand'? What are the guardrail bounds? What does 'real time' mean quantitatively? What is the pricing curve shape? Without these, the system cannot be correctly specified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Engineering", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A) Conduct structured requirements workshop with CEO, Finance, and Product to define all parameters",
        "B) Make assumptions based on industry norms (e.g., Uber-style surge pricing) and validate later",
        "C) Build a configurable framework where all parameters are externalized and tunable"
      ],
      "recommendation": "A combined approach: Option C for architecture (everything configurable), but Option A must happen before go-live to set correct initial values. Never ship with assumed guardrails."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory/legal constraints. Dynamic pricing in delivery/logistics may be subject to anti-price-gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and fair pricing requirements that vary by jurisdiction. This cannot be a pure product decision.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Engage Legal immediately to map jurisdictional constraints before defining guardrails",
        "B) Launch only in jurisdictions with no known pricing regulations and expand later"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Legal review must happen before guardrail values are finalized. Price gouging violations carry severe penalties and reputational damage."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No mention of how dynamic pricing affects courier/driver compensation. If delivery fees increase but courier pay stays flat, this creates labor tension. If courier pay scales with price, the margin model changes significantly.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "Finance", "Operations"],
      "options": [
        "A) Courier pay is independent of dynamic pricing — company captures all surplus",
        "B) Courier pay includes a percentage of the surge premium — incentivizes availability during high demand",
        "C) Courier pay uses its own demand-based model independent of customer pricing"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B is industry standard (Uber, DoorDash model) and critical for ensuring courier supply during high-demand periods. Without it, high prices with no drivers defeats the purpose."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
